nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoon and seaward reefs at 0–16 m depth, apparently endemic to Tonga

diet.notes: Has been reported to accept finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and frozen herbivore preparations. Generally advised to feed small portions 2–3 times daily; supplementing with marine algae or spirulina has been recommended.

summary: The Green Canary Blenny (Meiacanthus tongaensis) is a venomous fang blenny apparently endemic to Tonga, generally considered reef-safe and suitable for established small to medium saltwater aquariums. It is noted for a distinctive hovering swimming style and a bright green head with a black-bordered dorsal fin.
careNotes:
The Green Canary Blenny is generally considered hardy and suitable for intermediate marine keepers, though it tends to require a mature, well-established tank with abundant live rock for grazing and shelter. It possesses small venomous canine teeth that it uses defensively — bites from hand-feeding or perceived threats have been reported as painful, and the species has been known to bite the inside of an attacker's mouth as a predator deterrent. Hand-feeding is best avoided, and tanks accessible to curious children should be covered or positioned with care.

The species has been reported to be a capable jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised. Unlike most blennies, which spend their time close to the substrate, the Green Canary Blenny tends to occupy mid-water, exhibiting a distinctive hovering-and-darting swimming style.

It is best kept singly or as a mated pair. The species tends to be territorial toward conspecifics (other members of the same species) and similarly shaped fang blennies, so keeping more than one in a tank is generally not recommended unless a confirmed mated pair is available. Care-level assessments vary across sources, ranging from beginner-accessible to moderate; intermediate is a reasonable middle ground for most hobbyists approaching this species for the first time.

Males tend to be larger than females and have been observed to undergo color changes during courtship and spawning.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity in the manner typical of Meiacanthus blennies, with males larger than females and displaying intensified coloration and color shifts during courtship. Eggs are demersal and adhesive; males have been observed to guard clutches. Larvae have been reported to require live rotifers as initial food, making captive rearing moderately challenging.

additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), adult size (4.5"/11.4 cm), care level (beginner — disagreed with LiveAquaria's moderate, averaged to intermediate), temperament (peaceful — disagreed with LiveAquaria's semi-aggressive, averaged to semi-aggressive), and lifespan (4–6 years). Confirmed jumping risk and venomous fangs.
